messages.content:
You are a question answering assistant. Your job is to generate a SPARQL query to answer a given user question.

You should follow a step-by-step approach to generate the SPARQL query:
1. Determine possible entities and properties implied by the user question.
2. Search for the entities and properties in the knowledge graphs. Where applicable, constrain the searches with already identified entities and properties.
3. Gradually build up the SPARQL query using the identified entities and properties. Start with simple queries and add more complexity as needed. Execute intermediate queries to get feedback and to verify your assumptions. You may need to refine or rethink your current plan based on the query results and go back to step 2 if needed, possibly multiple times.
4. Use the answer or cancel function to finalize your answer and stop the generation process.

Available knowledge graphs:
- "wikidata" at https://qlever.dev/api/wikidata
  - "entity" index (type="fuzzy"): Entities indexed by their labels and synonyms
  - "property" index (type="embedding", modalities="text"): Properties indexed by their labels, synonyms, and identifiers

Index types used:
- "embedding": Retrieves items by cosine similarity between their embeddings and the query embedding. The embedding model used depends on the index and may support text, images, or both.
- "fuzzy": Retrieves items by overlap between their label words and the query keywords. The query keywords must not match label words exactly, but some fuzziness is allowed. The longer a query keyword is, the more it can deviate from a label word and still be considered a match, though it will also contribute less to the overall score. No special query operators like AND/OR are supported.

SPARQL prefixes for use in function calls:
- bd: http://www.bigdata.com/rdf#
- cc: http://creativecommons.org/ns#
- data: https://www.wikidata.org/wiki/Special:EntityData/
- dc: http://purl.org/dc/elements/1.1/
- dct: http://purl.org/dc/terms/
- foaf: http://xmlns.com/foaf/0.1/
- geo: http://www.opengis.net/ont/geosparql#
- geof: http://www.opengis.net/def/function/geosparql/
- gn: http://www.geonames.org/ontology#
- hint: http://www.bigdata.com/queryHints#
- imdb: https://www.imdb.com/
- math: http://www.w3.org/2005/xpath-functions/math#
- ontolex: http://www.w3.org/ns/lemon/ontolex#
- owl: http://www.w3.org/2002/07/owl#
- p: http://www.wikidata.org/prop/
- pq: http://www.wikidata.org/prop/qualifier/
- pqn: http://www.wikidata.org/prop/qualifier/value-normalized/
- pqv: http://www.wikidata.org/prop/qualifier/value/
- pr: http://www.wikidata.org/prop/reference/
- prn: http://www.wikidata.org/prop/reference/value-normalized/
- prov: http://www.w3.org/ns/prov#
- prv: http://www.wikidata.org/prop/reference/value/
- ps: http://www.wikidata.org/prop/statement/
- psn: http://www.wikidata.org/prop/statement/value-normalized/
- psv: http://www.wikidata.org/prop/statement/value/
- qb: http://purl.org/linked-data/cube#
- qfn: http://qlever.cs.uni-freiburg.de/function#
- ql: http://qlever.cs.uni-freiburg.de/builtin-functions/
- qlss: https://qlever.cs.uni-freiburg.de/spatialSearch/
- rdf: http://www.w3.org/1999/02/22-rdf-syntax-ns#
- rdfs: http://www.w3.org/2000/01/rdf-schema#
- ref: http://www.wikidata.org/reference/
- s: http://www.wikidata.org/entity/statement/
- schema: http://schema.org/
- skos: http://www.w3.org/2004/02/skos/core#
- v: http://www.wikidata.org/value/
- void: http://rdfs.org/ns/void#
- wd: http://www.wikidata.org/entity/
- wdno: http://www.wikidata.org/prop/novalue/
- wdt: http://www.wikidata.org/prop/direct/
- wdtn: http://www.wikidata.org/prop/direct-normalized/
- wikibase: http://wikiba.se/ontology#
- xsd: http://www.w3.org/2001/XMLSchema#

Additional rules to follow:
- Explain your thought process before and after each step and function call.
- Do not ask the user for clarification until you have made an initial attempt at completing the task. When the task input is incomplete or ambiguous, proceed based on reasonable assumptions.
- Do not use "SERVICE wikibase:label { bd:serviceParam wikibase:language ..." in SPARQL queries. It is unsupported by the used QLever SPARQL endpoints. Use rdfs:label or similar properties to get labels instead.
- Always execute your final SPARQL query before giving an answer to make sure it returns the expected results.
- The SPARQL query should always return the actual identifiers / IRIs of the items in its result. It additionally may return labels or other human-readable information, but they are optional and should be put within optional clauses unless explicitly requested by the user.
- Do not stop early if there are still obvious improvements to be made to the SPARQL query. For example, keep refining your SPARQL query if its result contains irrelevant items or is missing items you expected.
- Do not perform additional computation (e.g. filtering, sorting, calculations) on the result of the SPARQL query to determine the answer. All computation should be done solely within SPARQL.
- For questions with a "True" or "False" answer the SPARQL query should be an ASK query.
What color is the mascot of the 2007 AFL draft club who 's player is younger brother of former Collingwood and Fremantle player , Chris Tarrant

content.message:
I need to find the color of the mascot for the 2007 AFL draft club whose player is the younger brother of Chris Tarrant.

Let me break this down step by step:

1. First, I need to find Chris Tarrant's younger brother
2. Then, identify which AFL club drafted that player in 2007
3. Finally, find the color of the mascot for that club

First, let me search for Chris Tarrant in Wikidata to get his details:

tool_calls.name: search_entity
tool_calls.result:
Top 10 entity alternatives:
1. Chris Tarrant (wd:Q2944522), also known as "Christopher John Tarrant":
  - English radio and television broadcaster
  - has occupation radio personality
  - has occupation television presenter
  - is instance of human
2. Chris Tarrant (wd:Q5108226):
  - footballer
  - has occupation Australian rules football player
  - is instance of human
3. Chris Yarran (wd:Q5108537), also known as "Christopher Yarran":
  - Australian rules footballer
  - has occupation Australian rules football player
  - is instance of human
4. Chris Tarrant: Extreme Railways (wd:Q65091508):
  - British television documentary series
  - is instance of television series
5. Chris Barratt (wd:Q116475278):
  - has occupation rugby league player
  - is instance of human
  - rugby league footballer
6. Chris Durrant (wd:Q62575908):
  - has occupation university student
  - is instance of human
7. Chris Garratt (wd:Q83714776):
  - has occupation researcher
  - is instance of human
  - researcher
8. Chris Warren (wd:Q1198389):
  - American actor
  - has occupation actor
  - has occupation ballet dancer
  - has occupation film actor
  - has occupation singer
  - has occupation television actor
  - is instance of human
9. Chris Bryant (wd:Q266146), also known as "Christopher Bryant", "Christopher John Bryant", "Sir Chris Bryant", "Sir Christopher John Bryant":
  - British politician and MP (born 1962)
  - has occupation politician
  - has occupation priest
  - has occupation screenwriter
  - has occupation writer
  - is instance of human
10. Chris Harman (wd:Q730190):
  - British journalist and political activist (1942-2009)
  - has occupation economist
  - has occupation editor
  - has occupation journalist
  - has occupation philosopher
  - has occupation sociologist
  - is instance of human

content.message:
I see there are multiple Chris Tarrants. The one who is an Australian rules football player is likely the one we want since the question mentions Collingwood and Fremantle (AFL clubs).

Let me search for properties related to birth or family for this Chris Tarrant to find his younger brother:

tool_calls.name: search_property_of_entity
tool_calls.result:
Top 10 property alternatives:
1. spouse (wd:P26 as wdt/p, matched via "husband"), also known as "common-law husband", "common-law spouse", "common-law wife", "husband", "husbands", etc.:
  - the subject has the object as their spouse (husband, wife, ... Use "unmarried partner" (P451) for non-married companions
2. given name (wd:P735 as wdt/p, matched via "middle name"), also known as "Christian name", "first name", "firstname", "forename", "middle name", etc.:
  - first name or another given name of this person; values used with the property should not link disambiguations nor family names
3. family name (wd:P734 as wdt/p, matched via "fam name"), also known as "clan name", "fam name", "last name", "lastname", "surname":
  - part of full name of person
4. date of birth (wd:P569 as wdt/p, matched via "birthyear"), also known as "birth date", "birthdate", "birth year", "birthyear", "born", etc.:
  - date on which the subject was born
5. instance of (wd:P31 as wdt/p, matched via "is an"), also known as "example of", "has type", "is a", "is an", "is of type", etc.:
  - type to which this subject corresponds/belongs. Different ... is an instance of mountain; volcano is a subclass of mountain
6. start of work period (wd:P2031 as wdt/p, matched via "years active"), also known as "active since", "career began", "career start", "date of activity (start)", "debut", etc.:
  - start of period during which a person or group flourished (fl. = "floruit") in their professional activity
7. place of birth (wd:P19 as wdt/p, matched via "born"), also known as "birth city", "birth location", "birth place", "birthplace", "born", etc.:
  - most specific known birth location of a person, animal or fictional character
8. Library of Congress authority ID (wd:P244 as wdtn/wdt/p, matched via "P244"), also known as "Authority LCCN", "LCAuth ID", "LCAuth identifier", "LCCN", "LC Control Number", etc.:
  - Library of Congress name authority (persons, families, ... by 8-10 digits (see regex). For manifestations, use P1144]
9. country of citizenship (wd:P27 as wdt/p, matched via "citizen of"), also known as "citizen of", "citizenship", "country", "(legal) nationality", "nation", etc.:
  - the object is a country that recognizes the subject as its citizen
10. award received (wd:P166 as wdt/p, matched via "win"), also known as "award", "awarded", "awards", "awards received", "award won", etc.:
  - award or recognition received by a person, organization or creative work

content.message: I don't see "younger brother" as a property option. Let me try searching for properties related to family relationships:

tool_calls.name: search_property
tool_calls.result:
Top 10 property alternatives:
1. sibling (wd:P3373 as wdt/p/ps/pq/pr/wdtn/psn/pqn/prn/psv/pqv/prv, matched via "brother"), also known as "bro", "brother", "brother of", "brother or sister", "brothers", etc.:
  - the subject and the object have at least one common parent ... etc.) and step-siblings (step-brothers, step-sisters, etc.)
2. relative (wd:P1038 as wdt/p/ps/pq/pr/wdtn/psn/pqn/prn/psv/pqv/prv, matched via "brother-in-law"), also known as "ancestor", "aunt", "aunt-in-law", "brother-in-law", "co-brother-in-law", etc.:
  - family member (qualify with "kinship to subject", P1039; for direct family member please use specific property)
3. father (wd:P22 as wdt/p/ps/pq/pr/wdtn/psn/pqn/prn/psv/pqv/prv), also known as "child of", "dad", "daddy", "daughter of", "has father", etc.:
  - male parent of the subject. For stepfather, use "stepparent" (P3448)
4. stepparent (wd:P3448 as wdt/p/ps/pq/pr/wdtn/psn/pqn/prn/psv/pqv/prv, matched via "stepfather"), also known as "stepchild of", "step father", "step-father", "stepfather", "step mother", etc.:
  - subject has the object as their stepparent
5. child (wd:P40 as wdt/p/ps/pq/pr/wdtn/psn/pqn/prn/psv/pqv/prv, matched via "parent of"), also known as "children", "daughter", "daughters", "descendants", "father of", etc.:
  - subject has object as child. Do not use for stepchildren—use "relative" (P1038), qualified with "kinship to subject" (P1039)
6. mother (wd:P25 as wdt/p/ps/pq/pr/wdtn/psn/pqn/prn/psv/pqv/prv, matched via "parent"), also known as "child of", "daughter of", "has mother", "is child of", "is daughter of", etc.:
  - female parent of the subject. For stepmother, use "stepparent" (P3448)
7. parent (wd:P8810 as wdt/p/ps/pq/pr/wdtn/psn/pqn/prn/psv/pqv/prv), also known as "guardian", "parents":
  - parent of the subject. Only use if neither father (P22) nor mother (P25) nor stepparent (P3448) is applicable.
8. replaces (wd:P1365 as wdt/p/ps/pq/pr/wdtn/psn/pqn/prn/psv/pqv/prv, matched via "forefather"), also known as "absorbed", "absorbs", "continues", "continues from", "deprecates", etc.:
  - person, state or item replaced. Use "structure replaces" ... was not replaced or predecessor and successor are identical
9. unmarried partner (wd:P451 as wdt/p/ps/pq/pr/wdtn/psn/pqn/prn/psv/pqv/prv, matched via "boyfriend"), also known as "boyfriend", "cohabitant", "domestic partner", "enbyfriend", "girlfriend", etc.:
  - someone with whom the person is in a relationship without being married. Use "spouse" (P26) for married couples
10. family (wd:P53 as wdt/p/ps/pq/pr/wdtn/psn/pqn/prn/psv/pqv/prv), also known as "dynasty", "house", "member of family", "noble family", "royal house":
  - family, including dynasty and nobility houses. Not family name (use P734 for family name).

content.message: Great! I found that "sibling" (wd:P3373) is the property for brother/sister relationships. Let me search for the younger brother of the Australian rules footballer Chris Tarrant:

I need to try a different approach. Let me first verify which Chris Tarrant is the Australian rules footballer by looking at his properties:
<tool_call>
{"name": "search_with_constraints", "arguments": "kg": "wikidata", "index": "entity", "position": "subject", "query": "Chris Tarrant", "constraints": {"property": "wdt:P106"}}
</tool_call>

question: What color is the mascot of the 2007 AFL draft club who 's player is younger brother of former Collingwood and Fremantle player , Chris Tarrant
